query: effectiveness of multipotent adult progenitor cells in stroke treatment

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Neuron Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Use of stem cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: The proposed mechanism of the utility of stem cell therapy includes the differentiation of stem cells into spinal neurons that synapse with existing motor neurons to establish or maintain neurocircuitry and provide neurotrophic support. Differentiation of stem cells into non-neuronal cells can also impact disease progression by providing neurotrophic support, preventing oligodendrocyte dysfunction and toxicity. Peripheral stem cell transplantation can maintain the neuromuscular junction. The immunomodulatory effect of mesenchymal stem cells mobilized from the bone marrow can also attenuate the inflammatory response via the production of anti-inflammatory mediators. The commonly used stem cell therapies include olfactory ensheathing stem cells, endogenous mesenchymal stem cells, autologous mesenchymal stem cells, and neural progenitor cells. The most significant number of trials of stem cell therapy involves the use of mesenchymal stem cells. [94]

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Stem Cell Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Marrow stromal cells (MSCs) are under investigation as a treatment for neurodegenerative trinucleotide repeat disorders that are often fatal, particularly Huntington disease. The therapy is being tested in FDA-approved phase I to III clinical trials for various disorders following promising results from extensive preclinical studies on neurodegenerative conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelizaeus-Merzbacher Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A clinical trial by Gupta et al. demonstrated good results of intracerebral injection of neural stem cells in 2 subjects of PMD. The results were consistent with remyelination. The first subject, though, did not have significant neurologic improvement, experienced an improvement in his continuous positive airway pressure support (recall that patients with severe PMD have stridor). The second subject had improved truncal support, ambulation, and speech. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Increasing Awareness about Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension Treatment for Nurses (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Emerging Therapies and Treatment Pathways
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to in situ proliferation of vascular cells, studies over the past 20 years have revealed a role for circulating and resident vascular cells in pulmonary vascular remodeling, unveiling therapeutic potential for circulating EPCs and mesenchymal stem cells. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Stem cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Stem cell treatments for spinal cord injuries can be categorized into 2 main types: supportive and regenerative therapies. Supportive stem cell therapy employs nonneural stem cells, such as mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) derived from bone marrow, umbilical cord, or adipose tissue. These cells are administered intravenously or intrathecally due to limited migration to the target area and differentiation into neural cells. MSCs release neurotrophic factors that can repair the injured area; however, their ability to replenish the nervous system is limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Stem Cell Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Potential benefits arise from the innate trophic properties of MSCs or the delivery of enhanced growth factors, such as brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and glial-derived neurotrophic factor (GDNF). These factors, introduced into the brain using MSCs as delivery vehicles, reduce free radical damage through paracrine actions, support synaptic connections, and promote neuronal survival and regeneration. Current protocols highlight the applicability of MSC-based cellular therapies for treating trinucleotide repeat disorders. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Injuries -- Treatment / Management -- Stem cell therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Most clinical trials focus on this therapy due to the easier MSC preparation and regulatory compliance. In contrast, loading therapy employs stem cells capable of producing neural cells, such as olfactory ensheathing cells and neural progenitor cells, and stem cells derived from embryonic stem cells. Engraftment of these cells has a greater likelihood of replacing lost nerve cells in terms of functionality, though the procedure requires invasive transplantation methods and complex cell preparation processes. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Neurotmesis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Incorporated seed cells and neurotrophic factors further enhance the therapeutic effect of the biomaterial-based scaffold. [33] The incorporation of stem cells, such as embryonic stem cells, neural stem cells, bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells, adipose stem cells, skin-derived precursor stem cells, and induced pluripotent stem cells has enhanced the therapeutic effects of tissue-engineered nerve grafts. [16] Despite the encouraging repairing effects of neural stem cells, the clinical use of neural stem cells may be limited by the difficulty in collecting them, and the possibility of tumor formation. Bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells can differentiate to Schwann-like cells and boost neurite outgrowth when co-cultured with neurons. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Umbilical Cord -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Mesenchymal stem cells found within the Wharton’s jelly of the umbilical cord express c-kit and telomerase activity, consistent with the markers of stem cells. [9] [10] [11] These cells can be easily extracted after delivery and offer a source of stem cells with fewer ethical considerations than other sources. [7] [10] These cells have shown the ability to differentiate into neurons and glia when exposed to specific growth factors. [9] Additionally, these cells may play a role in treating autoimmune disease due to their ability to suppress the secretion of interferon-gamma and transform growth factor-beta1. [10] A more recent study completed on rats showed that transplantation of Wharton’s jelly into the site of traumatic brain injury could reduce the amount of brain damage by reducing brain edema and increasing expression of brain-derived neurotrophic factor. [7] Researchers continue to investigate Wharton’s jelly and mesenchymal stem cells for their potential therapeutic and technological roles. [10] [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Medical Oncology -- Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant
retrieved_docs.content: Alloimmunization due to repetitive transfusions (exchange of blood) Organ dysfunction due to SCA (possibly a reason why younger patients do better) Lack of matched sibling donors/ insurance. Indications for performing allogeneic HSCT Stroke (most common and strongest indication to perform allogeneic HSCT. Abnormal transcranial doppler Acute chest syndrome Recurrent VOC not controlled with medical therapy or chronic transfusions The complications with allogeneic HSCT: Transplant-related mortality approaches 7 to 10%, comparable with SCD-related mortality Graft rejection OR graft failure - less with myeloablative regimens (7 to 11%) compared to non-myeloablative regimens (11 to 50%) Graft-versus-host disease and related morbidity Transplant-related complications like lung injury, endocrine, and metabolic adverse events

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Eluting Stent Compounds -- Issues of Concern -- The Therapeutic Agent
retrieved_docs.content: Stem-cell carrying stents to support healthy reendothelialization

retrieved_docs.title: Regenerative Therapy in Pain -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Regenerative therapy is generally well tolerated. The most common side effects are injection site swelling, pain or soreness, or stiffness. [20] [21] Therapy-specific complications include hemarthrosis, septic arthritis, seizures, pulmonary embolism, and anaphylactic reactions. However, these more severe side effects are extremely rare. Stem cell therapy is very well tolerated, with most patients reporting satisfaction with the procedure. Other reported side effects include low-grade fevers. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Function -- Stroke Center Classification
retrieved_docs.content: For a facility to be designated as a TSC, it must perform a specified minimum number of MT each year. TSCs are designed to ensure all the capabilities of PSCs while additionally possessing the capacity to deliver high-quality endovascular thrombectomy (EVT) and post-EVT care. In addition to their ability to perform MT, TSCs are required to establish transfer agreements with CSCs. These agreements facilitate the swift transfer of patients who encounter intracranial hemorrhages or present with cases that surpass the resources available at the TSC.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurotmesis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Adipose stem cells can be induced into spindle-shaped cells that express Schwann cell markers, secrete neurotrophic factors, stimulate neurite outgrowth and form myelin sheaths, skin-derived precursor stem cells, and induced pluripotent stem cells. The downsides of induced pluripotent stem cells are that they exhibit some similar characteristics to embryonic stem cells, including malignant potential. Immunomodulation to promote myelin debris clearance during Wallerian degeneration and promote subsequent axon regeneration via enhanced macrophage recruitment in neurotmesis has been used in combination with mesenchymal stem cells. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation in Sickle Cell Disease -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: It is generally agreed that HSCT is indicated in children with progressive organ dysfunction, such as worsening cardiac, renal, and pulmonary function, recurrent stroke, and recurrent priapism, despite the institution of optimal medical therapy. HSCT can also be considered in older adolescents and young adults who can make an informed decision regarding the risks and benefits of the procedure. Regardless of the patient's age, the availability of a suitable donor, such as a fully matched sibling, is an essential requirement. [12] Alternate donors, such as haploidentical and matched unrelated donors, may be considered in selected cases. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Omental Tumors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Stem cells within the omentum promote angiogenesis by producing vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and fibroblast growth factor (FGF). Inflammation and hypoxia can drive VEGF expression; these cytokines promote the migration of neural stem cells to the injury site. Laminin within the omental stroma produces vasoactive growth factor and neurotrophic factor. Mechanisms have been proposed for the role of the omentum in stroke recovery, treatment for Alzheimer Disease, and increasing perfusion to ischemic limbs. [2]

retrieved_docs.content: Late effects of cerebrovascular disease has parent اضطراب مخي وعائي, indicating that the long‑term sequelae of cerebrovascular disease are classified under the broader category of cerebrovascular disorder. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians organize diagnoses, anticipate chronic complications, and tailor long‑term management strategies for patients who have experienced cerebrovascular events.

By consistently applying these qualifiers across diverse diseases, the framework supports comprehensive assessment, integrated therapeutic decision‑making, and holistic patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: distinguishing non‑infarcting stenotic disease. The clinical course descriptor “Transitory” is applied to transient cerebral ischemia, which corresponds to a transient ischemic attack (نوبة إقفارية عابرة), highlighting the temporary nature of some ischemic events. Overall, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries is a pivotal pathological mechanism that can lead to multiple forms of cerebral infarction, may be asymptomatic, is associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis, and is integrated within a structured classification of cerebrovascular circulatory diseases. These relationships inform diagnosis, risk assessment, therapeutic decision‑making (including endarterectomy), and the broader understanding of disease hierarchy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin, an anticoagulant medication, may be employed in the management of احتشاء دماغي (cerebral infarction or stroke). By inhibiting clot formation, heparin can help prevent the propagation of thrombi in patients experiencing this type of brain injury, underscoring its therapeutic role in acute stroke care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2445
retrieved_docs.content: Other cerebrovascular diseases (Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten) comprise a range of distinct conditions, including dissection of cerebral arteries without rupture (Dissectie van cerebrale arteriën zonder ruptuur) and brain injury caused by excessive blood pressure (اعتلال دماغي ناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم). Both of these entities are classified as child conditions of the broader category of other cerebrovascular diseases, illustrating the diversity of non‑hemorrhagic vascular pathologies that can affect the brain. Recognizing these specific subtypes within Overige cerebrovasculaire ziekten is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted therapeutic strategies in cerebrovascular patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: underscoring the extensive network of conditions—genetic, vascular, developmental, traumatic, neoplastic, and CSF‑related—that converge on migraine as a central node within neurological health and patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology of diverse neurologic disorders and underscore the need for precise classification, thorough neurologic evaluation, and targeted management to guide diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 188
retrieved_docs.content: arterial occlusion, stenosis, thrombosis, embolism, and chronic atherosclerosis converge to produce both acute cerebral infarcts and transient ischemic attacks, underscoring their relevance for diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of cerebrovascular disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2354
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria basilaris is identified as the anatomical location where transient cerebral ischemia occurs. Transient cerebral ischemia, also referred to by its Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, represents a brief episode of reduced cerebral blood flow that can produce temporary neurological deficits. Recognizing that transient cerebral ischemia originates in the basilar artery is important for clinicians, as ischemic events in this posterior circulation can lead to distinct clinical presentations and influence diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2087
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease and late effects of cerebrovascular disease are both classified under the broader category اضطراب مخي وعائي (cerebrovascular disorder). Within this hierarchy, other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease is specifically linked to تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ (fibrous sclerosis), indicating that some poorly defined cerebrovascular conditions may involve fibrotic changes. Recognizing that these subtypes fall under اضطراب مخي وعائي and that other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease can be associated with fibrous sclerosis helps clinicians organize cerebrovascular diagnoses and anticipate potential fibrotic complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: developmental disorders, and traumatic brain injury constitute an interconnected spectrum of nervous‑system disorders that inform comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1058
retrieved_docs.content: peripheral nerve involvement is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, monitoring and comprehensive patient care across the diverse disorders classified under Other disorders of the nervous system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 949
retrieved_docs.content: Central nervous system vascular diseases (SMQ) are classified as a subset of cerebrovascular accidents (حادثة وعائية دماغية). Cerebral artery sclerosis (تصلب الشرايين المخية) is closely linked to cerebrovascular accidents, serving as a contributing factor to stroke. In addition, cerebral artery sclerosis is associated with the development of multiple lacunar infarcts (خَرَف احتشائي متعدّد). These interconnections underscore the importance of arterial sclerosis in the pathogenesis of various cerebrovascular events and highlight its relevance for diagnosis, risk assessment, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2454
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System is the anatomical region implicated in various cerebrovascular and arterial disorders. It is identified as the anatomic site associated with other and ill-defined cerebrovascular disease, reflecting a range of poorly characterized cerebrovascular pathologies that involve the brain’s blood vessels. Additionally, the Vascular System is the site of تسلخ الشرايين, an arterial ulceration condition that denotes damage to the arterial wall. Recognizing the Vascular System as the common anatomical substrate for these conditions underscores its central role in the pathophysiology of cerebrovascular disease and arterial injury, informing diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral infarction (“الاحتشاء”) results from occlusion, stenosis, embolism or thrombosis of the precerebral arteries, including embolic or thrombotic precerebral artery infarcts and basilar‑artery occlusion, and is pathophysiologically linked to “انسداد الأوعية الدموية” (vascular occlusion). Asymptomatic basilar‑artery occlusion, although sometimes perceived as a state of general wellbeing, may present with transient faintness (“غشي”). Within the hierarchy of nervous‑system circulatory disorders, “Circulatory Diseases and Syndromes of Nervous System” contains the child “حادثة وعائية دماغية” (cerebrovascular accident) and the parent “transient cerebral ischemia” (TIA, “نوبة إقفارية عابرة”), the latter being a brief, reversible reduction in cerebral blood flow. Cerebral artery sclerosis (“تصلب الشرايين المخية”) and intracranial arterial sclerosis (“تصلب عصيدي في المخ”) contribute to chronic arterial stiffening, increase the risk of cerebrovascular accidents, and are associated with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.
